source_metadata.title_raw: H. Peter Klein - A-82-018 - January 25, 1982 - Ukiah

content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660                322-5901              322-6441              322-6444


January 25, 1982


H. Peter Klein
Deputy County Counsel
Courthouse
Ukiah, CA  95482

Dear Mr. Klein:

I am writing in response to your request for information from this office. You indicated in your letter that the County of Mendocino's Conflict of Interest Code designates the positions of County psychiatrist and psychologist. Employees holding these positions are required to disclose, in their Statements of Economic Interests, all investments and sources of income. These disclosure requirements are intended to prevent the diversion of County mental health exhibits to the psychiatrists' and psychologists' private practices or to clinics in which they have a financial interest.

Your concern is that these disclosure demands may improperly require the psychologists and psychiatrists to communicate the names of patients and clients which are ordinarily considered confidential and privileged. Similarly, you are concerned that the County's disclosure provisions for attorneys and social workers may improperly require them to disclose their private clients' names.

A private practice, such as one which may be engaged in by a psychiatrist, psychologist, attorney or social worker, constitutes a "business entity" for purposes of the Political Reform Act. The Act defines the term "business entity" to be "any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, ... corporation or association." (Government Code Section 87103(d)) (See also Hays v. Wood, (1979) 25 Cal.3d 772, 782-784.)

Because a professional's practice is a "business entity", under Mendocino County's Conflict of Interest Code and the provisions of the Political Reform Act, a psychiatrist or other professional with a private practice would only have to report the names of clients from whom the professional received a pro rata share of the fee (gross receipts) of

A-82-018

H. Peter Klein
January 25, 1982
Page Two


$10,000 or more during a calendar year.  (See Government
Code Sections 82030(a) and 87207(b)(3).)  Since the pro-
fessionals designated in the County's Code probably have
very few private clients who provide them with $10,000 in
annual gross receipts, few, if any, names would be dis-
closeable under these provisions.

     As to the names of those clients who do provide $10,000
or more, 2 California Administrative Code Section 18740
provides:

          An official need not disclose under Government
     Code Section 87207(b)(3) the name of a person who paid
     fees or made payments to a business entity if disclosure
     of a person's name would violate a legally recognized
     privilege under California law...."

     Evidence Code Sections 1010-1026 recognizes a psycho-
therapist-patient privilege.  The term "psychotherapist" is
defined in Section 1010 to mean:

          (a) A person authorized, or reasonably believed
     by the patient to be authorized, to practice medicine
     in any state or nation who devotes, or is reasonably
     believed to devote, a substantial portion of his time
     to the practice of psychiatry;

          (b) A person licensed as a psychologist...;1/

          (c) A person licensed as a clinical social worker
     ... when he is engaged in applied psychotherapy of a
     nonmedical nature;2/

          (d) A persçn who is serving as a school
     psychologist....;3/


_________________________

     1/  The person must be licensed under Chapter 6.6 (commencing
with Section 2900) of Division 2 of the Business and Professions
Code.

     2/  The person must be licensed under Article 4 (commencing
with Section 9040) of Chapter 17 of Division 3 of the Business
and Professions Code.

     3/  The person must hold a credential authorizing such
service issued by the state.

H. Peter Klein
January 25, 1982
Page Three


(e) A person licensed as a marriage, family and
child counselor....4/

The patient-psychotherapist privilege is construed
liberally in favor of the patient and his right to privacy
(In re Lifschutz (1970) 2 Cal.3d 415, 437) and it protects
the names of clients from disclosure (Smith v. Superior
Court (1981) 118 Cal. App.3d 136, 141-142). The court in
Smith stated:

...When a patient seeks out the counsel of a psycho-
therapist, he wants privacy and sanctuary from the
world and its pressures. The patient desires in this
place of safety an opportunity to be as open and candid
as possible to enable the psychotherapist the maximum
opportunity to help him with his problems. The patient's
purpose would be inhibited and frustrated if his psycho-
therapist could be compelled to give up his identity
without his consent. Public knowledge of treatment by
a psychotherapist reveals the existence and, in a general
sense, the nature of the malady.

(At 142.)

Because of this patient-psychotherapist privilege,
disclosure of psychotherapists' clients' names is not required
on Statements of Economic Interest. As a result, the State-
ments cannot serve the County's stated purpose in designating
the positions of County psychiatrist and psychologist, which
is to prevent the diversion of county mental patients into
private practices and clinics. Unless there is another
purpose for these designations, the County should consider
removing or modifying them.

As to disclosure requirements for County-employed attorneys,
California law recognizes an attorney-client privilege (Evidence
Code Sections 950-962), although it is not as broad as the
patient-psychotherapist privilege and it does not ordinarily
protect the client's identity. (Hays v. Wood (1979) 25 Cal.
3d 772, 785.) Only limited protection of clients' names is

________________________
4/ The person must be licensed under Chapter 4 (commencing
with Section 17800) of Part 3, Division 5 of the Business
and Professions Code.

H. Peter Klein
January 25, 1982
Page Four


provided by the privilege, such as in cases where disclosure
of the client's name might serve to make the client the
subject of an official investigation or expose him to criminal
or civil liability.  (Id.)

     Under the County's Conflict of Interest Code, the names
of private clients providing a county-employed attorney with
$10,000 in annual gross revenues are required to be disclosed.
If an attorney feels that a particular client's identity is
priviledged then he or she may apply to the Fair Political
Practices Commission for an exemption from disclosure under
the procedure outlined in 2 California Administrative Code
Section 18740.

     In regard to the County's designation of social workers,
Evidence Code Section 1010(c) extends the psychotherapist-
patient privilege to include certain licensed clinical social
workers when they are engaged in applied psychotherapy of a
nonmedical nature.  Other than this, there is no recognized
client-social worker privilege.  The names of the clients of
these other types of social workers would ordinarily be
discloseable, subject, of course, to the $10,000 gross revenue
limit.

     In conclusion, the $10,000 revenue limit and the applicable
privileges greatly minimize the disclosure of clients' names
under the Act.  Therefore, Mendocino County may want to rely
on its local rules in order to protect against the diversion
of clients to private practices.

     If I may be of any further help to you, please feel
free to contact me at 916/322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

JSM:km

OFFICE OF THE
COUNTY COUNSEL

JOHN A. DRUMMOND
COUNTY COUNSEL

H. PETER KLEIN
DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL

STEPHEN D. UNDERWOOD
DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL

RONALD R. BALL
DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL

COURTHOUSE
UKIAH, CALIFORNIA 95482
(707) 468-4446

October 26, 1981

Ms. Barbara Milman
F.P.P.C.
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Ms. Milman:

The County of Mendocino recently designated the
positions of psychiatrist and psychologist in the County's
Conflict of Interest Code. Employees holding these
positions are required to disclose all investments and
sources of income in their statements of Economic Interest.
The thoughts behind designating those positions was to
prevent the potential diversion of County mental health
patients to the private practice of the individual psychiatrist
or psychologist to a clinic in which these employees may
have an investment or ownership interest. The County Mental
Health Department has in place rules prohibiting the above
mentioned practices. Our concern is that the county may be
requiring the disclosure of the names of patients/clients
which are ordinarily considered confidential and privileged.
We foresee a similar problem with the County's clinical
social worker and attorney designations.

We understand the Commission is currently formulating a
policy/opinion regarding the disclosure of privileged
information. We have advised the affected employees that
they would not be required to report this information until
we hear from the FPPC. If this information is nondiscloseable
then there would be no reason for the medical/psychiatric
employees working for the County to be designated employees.

Another question we have involves interpretation of
2 CAC, Section 18730 (b)(4). The County of Mendocino is
desirous of requiring each special district in the County to
be its own filing officer. Is this possible under either
subsection A, B or C? If not possible under these sections

Barbara Milma
October 26, 1981
page 2


can provision be made under the individual codes designating
each special district as its own filing officer.

       Thank you for your assistance, please feel free to call
me if you have any questions.


                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        H. Peter Klein
                                        Deputy County Counsel

HPK/jk

sections.question: County of Mendocino designated the positions of psychiatrist and psychologist in the County's Conflict of Interest Code, requiring employees to disclose all investments and sources of income in Statements of Economic Interest. The concern is that the County may be requiring the disclosure of the names of patients/clients which are ordinarily considered confidential and privileged. A similar problem is foreseen with the County's clinical social worker and attorney designations. Additionally, the County seeks interpretation of 2 CAC Section 18730(b)(4) regarding whether each special district in the County can be its own filing officer.
sections.conclusion: Under the Statements of Economic Interest, disclosure of names is not required for clients below the $10,000 gross revenue threshold. For those exceeding this threshold, California Administrative Code Section 18740 provides that disclosure is not required if it would violate a legally recognized privilege. The psychotherapist-patient privilege (Evidence Code Sections 1010-1026) protects names of psychotherapy clients, so disclosure of psychotherapists' clients' names is not required. The attorney-client privilege is narrower but provides some protection. For social workers engaged in psychotherapy, the privilege applies. However, absent the privilege, other social workers' clients' names may be discloseable. The County should rely on the $10,000 revenue limit and applicable privileges to minimize disclosure of clients' names. If no other purpose exists for designating these positions, the County should consider removing or modifying them.
sections.question_synthetic: Does disclosure of patient and client names by psychiatrists, psychologists, social workers, and attorneys violate confidentiality and privilege protections? Can special districts in the County serve as their own filing officers?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Patient and client names are generally protected from disclosure through the $10,000 gross revenue threshold and recognized legal privileges (psychotherapist-patient privilege, attorney-client privilege). The County can rely on these mechanisms to prevent disclosure of confidential client information while maintaining conflict-of-interest oversight.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (October 26, 1981) and the FPPC response (January 25, 1982). The second question regarding special district filing officers (Section 18730(b)(4)) appears in the incoming request but is not addressed in the FPPC response letter, suggesting a possible incomplete extraction or multi-part response. The FPPC response is attributed to Janis Shank McLean, Counsel, Legal Division.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: County of Mendocino designated the positions of psychiatrist and psychologist in the County's Conflict of Interest Code, requiring employees to disclose all investments and sources of income in Statements of Economic Interest. The concern is that the County may be requiring the disclosure of the names of patients/clients which are ordinarily considered confidential and privileged. A similar problem is foreseen with the County's clinical social worker and attorney designations. Additionally, the County seeks interpretation of 2 CAC Section 18730(b)(4) regarding whether each special district in the County can be its own filing officer.

CONCLUSION: Under the Statements of Economic Interest, disclosure of names is not required for clients below the $10,000 gross revenue threshold. For those exceeding this threshold, California Administrative Code Section 18740 provides that disclosure is not required if it would violate a legally recognized privilege. The psychotherapist-patient privilege (Evidence Code Sections 1010-1026) protects names of psychotherapy clients, so disclosure of psychotherapists' clients' names is not required. The attorney-client privilege is narrower but provides some protection. For social workers engaged in psychotherapy, the privilege applies. However, absent the privilege, other social workers' clients' names may be discloseable. The County should rely on the $10,000 revenue limit and applicable privileges to minimize disclosure of clients' names. If no other purpose exists for designating these positions, the County should consider removing or modifying them.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Mendocino County's concern that designating psychiatrist, psychologist, and social worker positions in its Conflict of Interest Code may require improper disclosure of patient and client names protected by privilege. The letter explains that the $10,000 revenue threshold and recognized legal privileges (particularly the psychotherapist-patient privilege) adequately protect client confidentiality.
